text: The surgeon begins the hiatal hernia repair using the da Vinci XI system by locating the pars flaccida of the gastro-hepatic ligament. The vessel sealer extend is used to incise the ligament, and dissection is carried towards the right cruce. The assistant hand exchanges from holding the pars flaccida to holding the fat adjacent to the curl muscle, exposing the peritoneal covering on the cruce. The peritoneum over the curl muscle is incised with small bites to expose the muscle fibers, accessing the mediastinum.

The surgeon extends the peritoneal dissection along the cruce, using the assistant hand to retract the hernia sac edge. Blunt dissection is performed in the mediastinum, revealing areolar tissue in the avascular plane. The esophagus and hernia contents are mobilized with clear visualization of the right mediastinum. The posterior vagus nerve is visible within the fat, and care is taken to preserve it during dissection.

The surgeon focuses on the left cruce, carefully removing the phrenogastric fat pad to expose the edge of the left curl muscle. The anterior vagus nerve is visible in the tissue anterior to the esophagus, and the surgeon gently spreads within the left mediastinum using areolar planes to avoid injuring the esophagus or nerve during dissection.

The surgeon retracts the curl muscle with the left hand to optimize visualization for blunt dissection with the right hand. Attention is turned to the posterior dissection, identifying the posterior confluence of the cruse. The surgeon gently spreads over the left muscle fibers from the right side, pushing mediastinal structures upward with the left hand while ligating posterior attachments with the right.

The surgeon creates a clear retroesophageal window and passes a Penrose drain around the esophagus for retraction. The posterior dissection is refined, and the remaining mediastinal attachments on the left side are taken down to complete the mobilization of the esophagus. The esophagus is visibly mobilized with two to three centimeters of intra-abdominal length achieved, preparing for curl closure.

The surgeon begins closure by protecting the aorta with the left hand while driving a needle through the cruce with the right hand. The camera is adjusted for larger movements within the abdomen during suturing. The loop is stabilized on the curl muscle to facilitate the next suture bite, aiming for minimal tension closure of the hiatus. Multiple sutures may be laid for wider curl separation, ensuring the diaphragm approximates the esophagus circumferentially without compression.

The surgeon tightens the last suture bite, releasing tension on the esophagus to allow it to fill the entire space of the hiatus. The suture is locked by running it back on itself two to three times, completing the hiatal hernia repair.
